perturbation.original_text: b. “Confidential Information” shall mean any and all materials, records, data, documents, lists, writings, and information (whether in writing, printed, electronically stored, computerized, on disk or otherwise, including, but not limited to, all copies, summaries, analyses, drafts, and extracts) relating or referring in any manner to trade secrets (as currently defined under applicable law, including, but not limited to, the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act and any amendments or successor statutes) of a Banking Organization, as well as other non-public financial or proprietary information of a Banking Organization including, but not limited to, business reports, business plans, projections, income statements, profit and loss statements, business strategies and/or strategic plans, internal audits, sales 2 information, sales techniques, business costs, product pricing, budgets, research and development, intellectual property, software and/or computer programs, marketing strategies or ideas, marketing plans or materials, business development plans or strategies, records or information relating to customers or account holders, customer lists, inventions, and processes, systems, methods, documentation or devices which are unique or proprietary to the business of or services or products of a Banking Organization. Confidential Information shall not include information that: (i) is or becomes readily or publicly available, not as a result of a violation of this Agreement or other obligation of confidence, directly or indirectly, of the disclosing party; (ii) is developed by other parties, including, but not limited to, subsequent employers of the Employee, independent of the knowledge or resources of the Employee based upon his or her prior access to a Banking Organization’s Confidential Information; (iii) is independently developed after the Employee’s termination of employment without reference to or use of the Confidential Information or materials based thereon; (iv) is based on the Employee’s personal experience, knowledge, enterprise and expertise in the delivery of Banking and Financial Services and which is general knowledge concerning the industry, not specific to a Banking Organization; or (v) is disclosed pursuant to the requirements of a court, administrative agency or other governmental agency.
perturbation.changed_text: b. “Confidential Information” shall mean any and all materials, records, data, documents, lists, writings, and information (whether in writing, printed, electronically stored, computerized, on disk or otherwise, including, but not limited to, all copies, summaries, analyses, drafts, and extracts) relating or referring in any manner to trade secrets of a Banking Organization, as well as other non-public financial or proprietary information of a Banking Organization including, but not limited to, business reports, business plans, projections, income statements, profit and loss statements, business strategies and/or strategic plans, internal audits, sales 2 information, sales techniques, business costs, product pricing, budgets, research and development, intellectual property, software and/or computer programs, marketing strategies or ideas, marketing plans or materials, business development plans or strategies, records or information relating to customers or account holders, customer lists, inventions, and processes, systems, methods, documentation or devices which are unique or proprietary to the business of or services or products of a Banking Organization. Confidential Information shall not include information that: (i) is or becomes readily or publicly available, regardless of how it becomes available; (ii) is developed by other parties, including, but not limited to, subsequent employers of the Employee; (iii) is independently developed after the Employee’s termination of employment; (iv) is based on the Employee’s personal experience; or (v) is disclosed pursuant to the requirements of a court, administrative agency or other governmental agency.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'not as a result of a violation of this Agreement or other obligation of confidence, directly or indirectly, of the disclosing party', 'independent of the knowledge or resources of the Employee based upon his or her prior access to a Banking Organization’s Confidential Information' and 'without reference to or use of the Confidential Information or materials based thereon' and 'knowledge, enterprise and expertise in the delivery of Banking and Financial Services and which is general knowledge concerning the industry, not specific to a Banking Organization' from the definition of what is excluded from 'confidential information,' this alteration broadens the scope of permissible disclosure and use of information, which may contradict trade secret protections under the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation and outlines remedies for trade secret misuse. By broadening the exceptions to 'Confidential Information,' the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Act's intent to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of such information. Specifically, the removal of the language which states that confidential information shall 'not [be] as a result of a violation of this Agreement' means that any public knowledge, even if due to a breach of the agreement, is not protected. The act seeks to protect trade secrets even if they are disclosed through nefarious means.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2. Definitions :: 2023 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code (here) 2022 Indiana Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Indiana Code Title 24. Trade Regulation Article 2. Trademarks, Trade Names, and Trade Secrets Chapter 3. Trade Secrets 24-2-3-2. Definitions Universal Citation: IN Code § 24-2-3-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, limited liability company, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens trade secret protection, creating a contradiction with the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Specifically, the original text excludes information from the definition of 'Confidential Information' if it becomes publicly available due to a breach of the agreement, thereby maintaining its protected status even if disclosed improperly. The changed text removes this protection, stating that any information that becomes publicly available is no longer considered confidential, regardless of how it became available. This directly conflicts with the intent of the Indiana Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which aims to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets, even if such information becomes public knowledge through 'improper means,' as defined in Indiana Code § 24-2-3-2. The deletion of the clause protects the banking organization, as trade secrets are not necessarily protected if they are released to the public. The new change means that the trade secrets, once released to the public, are no longer protected.

perturbation.original_text: Employee shall devote his/her full working time and attention to the performance of those services for the Banking Organization which are assigned to him/her from time to time and to perform such services in a faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest of such Banking Organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee shall devote reasonable time and attention to the performance of those services for the Banking Organization which are assigned to him/her from time to time and to perform such services to the best of their ability.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'full working time and attention' to 'reasonable time and attention' introduces ambiguity. Removing 'faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest of such Banking Organization' significantly weakens the employee's duty of loyalty. This contradicts the common law duty of loyalty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The common law duty of loyalty requires an employee to act solely for the benefit of the employer in matters within the scope of employment. By changing the required level of effort and removing the explicit requirement to act in the employer's best interest, the modified text weakens this duty, potentially allowing the employee to prioritize personal interests or engage in activities that are not in the employer's best interest.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of loyalty | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of loyalty The duty of loyalty is one of the fiduciary duties owed by a company’s directors . The duty of loyalty requires the directors to place the interests of the company and the shareholders before any of their personal interests. The directors’ actions such as diverting corporate assets , opportunities, or information for personal gain can certainly violate their duty of loyalty. Additionally, the duty of loyalty can be also violated when a director usurps a corporate chance by taking advantage of information valuable to the company and instead gains profits from it for personal benefits. In addition, the duty of loyalty places additional responsibilities upon the directors. For instance, the directors are required to keep confidential, but not to disclose or misuse any information that they receive in their capacity as directors. Also, the directors need to report every conflict of interest no matter whether real or perceived, to the company. In the cases where conflicts do exist, the directors should disclose all relevant information. To avoid violations of the duty, the directors are supposed to disclose personal conflicts they may have to the board of directors so that a disinterested vote could be taken without them. Directors are also supposed to be prepared to present any opportunities relevant to the corporation to the board before trying to benefit from them. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law business organizations corporations wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the employee's duty of loyalty, creating a legal contradiction. The original text requires the employee to devote 'full working time and attention' and act in a 'faithful, honest and diligent manner and in the best interest' of the Banking Organization. The changed text reduces this to 'reasonable time and attention' and removes the explicit requirement to act in the employer's best interest. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines the duty of loyalty as requiring directors to 'place the interests of the company and the shareholders before any of their personal interests.' While this snippet refers to directors, the Law Explanation extends this duty to employees, stating that the common law duty of loyalty requires an employee to act solely for the benefit of the employer. The altered contract text weakens this duty, potentially allowing the employee to prioritize personal interests, thus contradicting the common law duty of loyalty.

perturbation.original_text: Employee understands and agrees, upon the Effective Time, that he/she will be an at-will employee of Bank and that this Agreement does not change or affect Employee’s employee-at-will status.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee understands and agrees, upon the Effective Time, that he/she will be an employee of Bank and that this Agreement provides certain protections and guarantees of continued employment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the 'at-will employee' to 'employee' and adding that the 'agreement provides certain protections and guarantees of continued employment' directly contradicts the principle of at-will employment, potentially creating an implied contract.
perturbation.law_citation: Orr v. Westminster Village North, Inc., 689 N.E.2d 712, 717 (Ind. 1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana adheres to the at-will employment doctrine, which allows employers to terminate employment for any reason that does not violate public policy, absent a contract or other agreement. By suggesting protections and guarantees of continued employment, the modified text creates an ambiguity that could undermine the employer's right to terminate the employee at will, thus conflicting with the established legal doctrine.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of at-will employment. The original text explicitly states the employee is 'at-will,' while the changed text suggests 'certain protections and guarantees of continued employment.' The Law Explanation states that Indiana follows the at-will employment doctrine. The modified text introduces ambiguity that could undermine the employer's right to terminate employment at will, creating a contradiction. While the provided snippets don't explicitly define at-will employment, the Law Explanation fills in that gap, and the change clearly moves away from the at-will principle. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because 'at-will employee' has a specific legal meaning, and the change introduces language that deviates from that meaning, potentially making the contract unenforceable as an at-will employment agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, in the event any of the provisions of these Sections are breached by Employee, the commencement of the applicable post-termination restriction will not begin until Employee is in full compliance with Section 5 and/or 6 .
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, in the event any of the provisions of these Sections are allegedly breached by Employee, the commencement of the applicable post-termination restriction may not begin until a court determines that Employee is in full compliance with Section 5 and/or 6.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'breached' to 'allegedly breached' and adding 'may not begin until a court determines,' the enforceability of the tolling provision becomes uncertain. It could contradict Indiana law regarding the enforcement of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana law on Enforceability of Non-Compete Agreements
perturbation.law_citation: H & R Block E. Enters., Inc. v. Moore, 973 N.E.2d 1193, 1200 (Ind. Ct. App. 2012)
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana courts will enforce non-compete agreements if they are reasonable and protect a legitimate business interest. By adding uncertainty about when the restriction period begins and requiring a court determination, the modified text creates a loophole that could be used to delay or avoid the enforcement of the non-compete agreement, potentially conflicting with Indiana's approach to balancing employer and employee interests in such agreements. The original text provides clear guidance on compliance and tolling; the altered text obfuscates this.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'breached' to 'allegedly breached' and the addition of 'may not begin until a court determines' introduces ambiguity regarding when the post-termination restriction begins. This undermines the enforceability of the non-compete agreement by creating a potential loophole, as explained in both the Perturbation and Law Explanations. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because the altered terms deviate from the clear guidance on compliance and tolling provided in the original text and could be used to delay or avoid the enforcement of the non-compete agreement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is limited, it points to the existence of legal opinions regarding court decisions, implying that the introduction of a court determination requirement alters the original obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is non-existent, so it can't be used.
